translations.de: Oh Allah, wir verbringen den Abend in Deiner Gegenwart, wir erwachen am Morgen in Deiner Gegenwart, wir leben durch Dich, wir sterben durch Dich, und zu Dir ist die Rückkehr.
translations.en-us: O God, we spend this evening in Your presence, we awaken in the morning in Your presence, we live by You, we die by You, and to You is our return.
translations.en-gb: O God, we spend this evening in Your presence, we awaken in the morning in Your presence, we live according to Your will, we die according to Your decree, and to You is our ultimate return.
translations.fr-fr: Ô Allah, nous passons la soirée en Votre présence, nous nous réveillons le matin avec Vous, nous vivons selon Vous, nous mourons selon Vous, et à Vous est notre retour.
translations.he: רִבוֹנוֹת הָעוֹלָמוֹת, אָנוּ מְבִילִים אֶת הָעֶרֶב בְּחִבָּתְךָ, וְקוּמִים בַּבֹּקֶר בְּאוֹרֶךָ, אָנוּ חַיִּים בְּסַנְטֶרֶךָ, וּמֵתִים בְּרָצוֹנְךָ, וְאֵלֶיךָ הַשֵּׂיבָה.
translations.hi: हे अल्लाह, हम शाम को तुम्हारे साथ बिताते हैं, सुबह तुम्हारे साथ जागते हैं, हम तुम्हारे द्वारा जीते हैं, तुम्हारे द्वारा मरते हैं, और तुम्हारे पास ही हम वापस लौटते हैं।
translations.id: Ya Allah, kami menghabiskan malam bersama-Mu, kami bangun di pagi hari bersama-Mu, kami hidup karena-Mu, kami mati karena-Mu, dan kepada-Mu lah kami kembali.
translations.it: O Allah, trascorriamo la sera in Tua compagnia, ci risvegliamo al mattino con Te, viviamo grazie a Te, moriamo con Te e a Te ritorneremo.
translations.ja:
夜はあなたと共に過ごし、朝はあなたと共に目覚め、あなたによって生き、あなたによって死に、そしてあなたへの帰還がある。

**アラビア語:**

يا الله، نقضي المساء معك، ونستيقظ في الصباح معك، نعيش بك، نموت بك، وإليك المصير.
translations.ko: 알라께서여, 우리는 저녁을 주님과 함께하며, 아침에 주님과 함께 깨어나고, 주님 안에서 살아가며, 주님께로 돌아가 죽습니다. 그리고 모든 것은 주님께로 돌아갑니다.
translations.nl: O Allah, we brengen de avond door met U, we ontwaken 's ochtends met U, we leven dankzij U, we sterven door U, en tot U keren wij terug.
translations.pl: O Allah, spędzamy ten wieczór w Twojej obecności, budzimy się rano w Twojej obecności, żyjemy dzięki Tobie, umieramy dzięki Tobie, a do Ciebie wracamy.
translations.pt-br: Ó Allah, passamos a noite em Tua presença, acordamos de manhã em Tua presença, vivemos por Ti, morremos por Ti, e a Ti retornamos.
translations.ru: О Аллах, мы проводим вечер в Твоем присутствии, мы просыпаемся утром в Твоем присутствии, мы живем благодаря Тебе, мы умираем благодаря Тебе, и к Тебе же мы возвращаемся.
translations.sv: "O Allah, vi tillbringar kvällen i Din närhet, vi vaknar på morgonen i Din närhet, vi lever genom Dig, vi dör genom Dig, och till Dig återvänder vi."
translations.th: โอ้ อัลลอฮฺ, พวกเราใช้เวลาช่วงเย็นอยู่กับพระองค์, พวกเราตื่นขึ้นในตอนเช้าอยู่กับพระองค์, พวกเราดำเนินชีวิตโดยพระองค์, พวกเราดับลมหายใจโดยพระองค์, และพวกเราจะกลับคืนสู่พระองค์.
translations.tr: Ey Allah'ım, biz akşamı Seninle geçiririz, sabahı Seninle uyanırız, Seninle yaşarız, Seninle ölürüz ve dönüşümüz Sensindir.
translations.uk: О Аллах, ми проводимо вечір у Твоєму товаристві, ми прокидаємося вранці з Тобою, ми живемо Твоїм життям, ми помираємо Твоїм смертю, і до Тебе ми повертаємося.
translations.vi: Lạy Allah, chúng tôi bắt đầu buổi tối với Ngài, chúng tôi thức dậy vào buổi sáng với Ngài, chúng tôi sống nhờ Ngài, chúng tôi chết bởi Ngài, và tất cả chúng tôi sẽ trở về với Ngài.
translations.zh-cn: 哎呀，我们与您同度晚，与您共迎晨，我们依靠您而生存，我们归于您而逝，最终归于您。

mantras.name: O my Lord, increase me in knowledge.
mantras.english: O my Lord, increase me in knowledge.
mantras.transliteration: ʾay Rabbī, zīd īnī ʿilman.
mantras.background: From the Qur'an, "Rabbi zidni ilma" (رب زدني علما) means "My Lord, increase me in knowledge" — a humble Islamic supplication inspiring lifelong, sincere learning.
mantras.benefits: "Rabbi Zidni Ilma" fosters humility, curiosity, and sincerity. Rooted in the Qur'an, it reframes knowledge-seeking as an act of worship, nurturing a lifelong, spiritually grounded pursuit of wisdom.
mantras.tags: islamic, quranic, allah, dhikr, supplication, knowledge, learning, humility, wisdom, sincere

translations.es: Oh, mi Señor, aumenta mi conocimiento.
translations.ar: يا رب، زدني علماً.
translations.bn: ওহে আমার প্রভু, আমাকে জ্ঞানে বৃদ্ধি করুন।
translations.de: "Oh mein Herr, erhöhe mich in Wissen."
translations.en-us: O my Lord, increase me in knowledge.
translations.en-gb: O my Lord, increase me in knowledge.
translations.fr-fr: Ô mon Seigneur, augmente ma connaissance.
translations.he: يا رب، زدني علما.
translations.hi: हे मेरे प्रभु, मुझे ज्ञान में वृद्धि प्रदान करो।
translations.id: Ya Tuhanku, tambahkanlah pengetahuanku.
translations.it: "O Signore, aumenta la mia conoscenza."
translations.ja: おお、主よ、私に知識をより多く与えてください。 (Ō, shujō, watashi ni chishiki o yori ōku ataete kudasai.)
translations.ko: 주님, 제 지혜를 더 늘려주소서.
translations.nl: O mijn Heer, vergroot mijn kennis.
translations.pl: O mój Panie, zwiększ mnie w wiedzy.
translations.pt-br: Ó meu Senhor, aumenta meu conhecimento.
translations.ru: О, мой Господь, увеличь меня в знаниях.
translations.sv: Å, Herre, öka min kunskap.
translations.th: โอ้ พระผู้เป็นเจ้า โปรดเพิ่มพูนความรู้แก่ข้า
translations.tr: Ey Rabbim, bilgimi artır.
translations.uk: О, мій Господи, збільш моє знання.
translations.vi: Lạy Chúa, xin hãy ban cho con thêm tri thức.
translations.zh-cn: 啊，我的主，请增加我知识。

mantras.name: O you who have faith, remember Allah with abundant remembrance.
mantras.english: O you who have faith, remember Allah with abundant remembrance.
mantras.original: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ ذِكْرًا كَثِيرًا
mantras.transliteration: `Yā ayyuha al-ladhīna āmanū, dhakiru Allāha dhikran kathīran.`
mantras.background: From the Quran, this call to "remember Allah with abundant remembrance" launched Dhikr — Islam's living tradition of mindful divine remembrance, practiced since Prophet Muhammad's time.
mantras.benefits: Dhikr—remembering Allah abundantly—cultivates sincerity, mindfulness, and emotional stability. Practitioners report reduced anxiety, deeper spiritual connection, and resilience. Hearts find satisfaction through its rhythmic remembrance, drawing believers into timeless peace.
mantras.tags: islamic, dhikr, allah, remembrance, devotion, mindfulness, peace, resilience, spiritual, sufi, recitation, focus

translations.es: Oh, vosotros que tenéis fe, recordad a Alá con abundante recuerdo.
translations.ar: يا أصحاب الإيمان، اذكّروا الله ذكرًا غزيرًا.
translations.bn: হে বিশ্বাসীগণ, তোমরা আল্লাহকে প্রচুর পরিমাণে স্মরণ করো।
translations.de: Ihr, die ihr den Glauben habt, erinnert Allah in reichem Maße.
translations.en-us: O you who believe, remember Allah frequently.
translations.en-gb: O you who have faith, remember Allah frequently and intensely.
translations.fr-fr: Ô vous qui avez la foi, invoquez Allah abondamment.
translations.he: הוי אלה המאמינים, זכרו את אללה בתפילה מרובה.
translations.hi: ऐ ईमान लाने वालों, अल्लाह को भरपूर याद करो।
translations.id: Wahai orang-orang yang beriman, ingatlah Allah dengan banyak mengingat-Nya.
translations.it: O voi che credete, ricordate Allah con una fervente commemorazione.
translations.ja: 信仰を持つあなたたちよ、アッラーを頻繁に、そして心から思い出しなさい。
translations.ko: 믿음을 가진 여러분, 하나님을 마음껏 기억하십시오.
translations.nl: O jullie die geloof hebben, gedenk Allah met overvloedige herinnering.
translations.pl: O wy, którzy wierzycie, pamiętajcie o Allahu poprzez liczne wspomnienia.
translations.pt-br: Ó vós que creis, recordai abundantemente a Allah.
translations.ru: О вы, уверовавшие, помните Аллаха часто и многократно.
translations.sv: O ni som tror, minns Allah genom riklig ihågkommande.
translations.th: ท่านผู้ศรัทธาทั้งหลาย จงรำลึกถึงอัลลอฮ์ด้วยการรำลึกที่มากมาย.
translations.tr: Ey iman edenler, Allah'ı bol bol zikredin.
translations.uk: О ви, які увірували, пам'ятайте Аллаха часто і щиро.
translations.vi: Hỡi những người có đức tin, hãy luôn nhớ đến Allah bằng lòng thành kính và sự biết ơn sâu sắc.
translations.zh-cn: 信仰之人，要常常铭记真主。

mantras.name: Salawat
mantras.english: O God, bestow Your blessings upon Muhammad
mantras.transliteration: Allāhumma ṣalli ʿalā Muḥammad
mantras.abstract:
The Salawat — blessings upon the Prophet — is among the most beloved of Islamic practices. The Quran itself (33:56) states that God and His angels send blessings upon the Prophet and commands believers to do the same. The act of sending Salawat is therefore a participation in a divine-angelic-human chorus of blessing, an alignment of the human heart with the love that God Himself has for His messenger.

The full Ibrahimic Salawat, recited in the final sitting of every prayer, extends the blessing to all of Muhammad's family and to the lineage of Ibrahim and his family, linking the entire Abrahamic tradition in a web of divine favour. This formula was gifted, according to tradition, in response to the companions' question of how to properly greet the Prophet.

In Sufi theology the Prophet Muhammad is understood as the first light created by God — the primordial reality (haqiqa Muhammadiyya) from which all of creation unfolds. Sending Salawat is thus not merely a historical act of remembering a seventh-century person but a metaphysical act of connecting to the principle of divine mercy and guidance that the Prophet embodies.

Many teachers prescribe Salawat as the most accessible entry point into love-based spirituality in Islam. Unlike fear-oriented practices that dwell on sin and judgment, Salawat orients the heart toward the beauty of divine mercy as personified in the Prophet. Sheikh Habib Umar bin Hafiz teaches that 1000 Salawat daily opens the heart to states of love that transcend ordinary emotional experience.

The effect most frequently reported by practitioners is an increase in love — for God, for the Prophet, for other human beings, and for oneself — alongside a reduction in hardness of heart (qaswat al-qalb), considered in Islamic psychology the root obstacle to spiritual development.

translations.en: O God, bestow Your blessings upon Muhammad
translations.zh: 真主啊，请赐福先知穆罕默德
translations.es: Oh Dios, bendice a Mahoma

mantras.name: Subhan Allah
mantras.english: Glory be to God
mantras.transliteration: Subḥāna llāh
mantras.abstract:
Subhan Allah is a formula of glorification (tasbih) expressing that God is utterly beyond all human imperfection, limitation, or comparison. The root 'sabaha' carries the image of swimming freely — God is free of all constraints, immaculate, transcendent. Reciting this phrase is an act of acknowledging divine perfection in contrast to the creaturely world.

The Prophet Muhammad is reported to have said that saying Subhan Allah, Alhamdulillah, and Allahu Akbar thirty-three times each after every prayer is equivalent in spiritual reward to a long night of voluntary worship. These three phrases together — the tasbihat — form a complete post-prayer dhikr cycle practised by hundreds of millions of Muslims daily.

In Sufi practice, Subhan Allah is used as a rhythmic breath mantra. Each exhalation carries 'subhan,' each inhalation 'Allah,' creating a loop of glorification that synchronizes breath and remembrance. Sheikh Ahmad Al-Alawi described this method as teaching the body to pray without ceasing, independent of the mind's chatter.

Naturally arising moments of beauty — a sunset, the birth of a child, an unexpected kindness — are traditional occasions to spontaneously utter Subhan Allah. The phrase marks the recognition that creation is a continuous outpouring of divine generosity and artistry.

Quantitatively, devotees often recite Subhan Allah 33 times on tasbih beads as part of the post-salah sequence. Advanced practitioners may recite it 100 or even 1000 times in sitting meditation, entering deep states of absorption where the phrase loses its conceptual surface and becomes pure vibration.

translations.en: Glory be to God
translations.zh: 真主至美无暇
translations.es: Gloria a Dios

mantras.name: The Wise Remembrance.
mantras.english: The Wise Remembrance.
mantras.transliteration: الذِّكْرُ الْحَكِيمُ
mantras.background: Dhikr (ذِكْر) means 'remembrance' in Arabic. Rooted in Islam & Sufism, it's the sacred act of repeatedly invoking God's names — awakening what the soul already knows.
mantras.benefits: Dhikr cultivates mindfulness of God through rhythmic repetition, anchoring the soul in divine presence. Practitioners report inner stillness, reduced heedlessness, and a rekindled awareness of innate spiritual knowledge — a living dialogue between human and divine.
mantras.tags: islamic, sufi, remembrance, dhikr, divine, reminder, prophet, tawhid, allah, revelation, faith, community, affirmation

translations.es: El Sabio Recuerdo.
translations.ar: الذكر الحكيم.
translations.bn: বিজ্ঞজনের স্মরণ।
translations.de: Die Weise Erinnerung.
translations.en-us: The Wise Remembrance.
translations.en-gb: The Wise Remembrance.
translations.fr-fr: Le Souvenir Sagesse.
translations.he: הזיכרון הנבון.
translations.hi: "बुद्धिमान स्मरण" या "विद्वान स्मरण"।
translations.id: Pengingat Kebijaksanaan.
translations.it: Il Saggio Ricordo.
translations.ja: 知恵に満ちた回想 (Chie ni michita kaizō)
translations.ko: 현명한 회상 (Hyeonmungan hoesang)
translations.nl: De wijze herinnering.
translations.pl: Mądre Wspomnienie.
translations.pt-br: A Memorável Sabedoria.
translations.ru: Мудрое воспоминание.
translations.sv: Det vise minnet.
translations.th: ความทรงจำอันชาญฉลาด.
translations.tr: Bilgeliğin Hatırlanması.
translations.uk: Мудре Згадування.
translations.vi: Sự Tưởng Nhớ Khôn Ngoan.
translations.zh-cn: 智慧的回忆 (Zhìhuì de huíyì)

mantras.name: There is no god but Allah, He is alone, He has no partner, to Him belongs the sovereignty, and to Him belongs the praise, and He is able to do all things.
mantras.english: There is no god but Allah, He is alone, He has no partner, to Him belongs the sovereignty, and to Him belongs the praise, and He is able to do all things.
mantras.original: Lā ilāha illa Allāh, waḥdahu lā sharīka lah, lahu al-mulk wa lahu al-ḥamd wa huwa ʿalā kulli shayʾin qadīr
mantras.transliteration: لا إله إلا الله، وهو أحد، له لا شريك، له الملك، وله الحمد، وهو على كل شيء قدير.
mantras.background: From Islamic dhikr tradition, this mantra means "No god but Allah, alone, no partner; His is all sovereignty, praise, and power" — recited 100x daily for profound spiritual reward.
mantras.benefits: Reciting this dhikr 100x daily is said to erase sins, shield against negative forces, and cultivate profound monotheistic focus—anchoring the mind in divine unity and sparking deep inner peace.
mantras.tags: islamic, allah, dhikr, devotional, affirmation, tawhid, protection, forgiveness, divine

translations.es: No hay más que Dios, Alá. Él es único, no tiene asociado, a Él pertenece la soberanía, a Él pertenece la alabanza, y Él es capaz de todas las cosas.
translations.ar: لا إله إلا الله، وحده لا شريك له، له الملك وله الحمد، وهو على كل شيء قدير.
translations.bn: আল্লাহ ব্যতীত অন্য কোনো উপাস্য নেই, তিনি এক, তাঁর কোনো শরীক নেই, সার্বভৌমত্ব তাঁরই, প্রশংসা তাঁরই, এবং তিনি সবকিছু করতে সক্ষম।
translations.de: Es gibt keinen Gott außer Allah, Er ist einzigartig, Er hat keinen Partner, Ihm gehört die Herrschaft, und Ihm gebührt aller Lob, und Er ist in der Lage, alles zu tun.
translations.en-us: There is no deity worthy of worship except Allah; He is alone, without any partner; to Him belongs all dominion, and to Him all praise is due; and He is capable of all things.
translations.en-gb: There is no deity but Allah; He is unique, having no partner; to Him belongs all dominion, and to Him all praise is due; and He is capable of all things.
translations.fr-fr: Il n'y a de divinité qu'Allah, Il est unique, Il n'a associé, à Lui seule appartient la souveraineté, et à Lui seule appartiennent les louanges, et Il est capable de toute chose.
translations.he: אין אלוהים אלא אללה, הוא לבדו, אין לו שותף, לו השליטה, ולו התהילה, והוא המסוגל לכל.
translations.hi: "अल्लाह के सिवा कोई ईश्वर नहीं है, वह अकेला है, उसका कोई साझीदार नहीं है, उसी के लिए प्रभुत्व है, और उसी से प्रशंसा आती है, और वह सब कुछ करने में सक्षम है।"
translations.id: Tidak ada Tuhan kecuali Allah, Dia Maha Esa, Dia tidak memiliki sekutu, kepadanya kekuasaan mutlak, dan hanya kepada-Nya segala pujian, dan Dia berkuasa atas segala sesuatu.
translations.it: Non c'è altro dio all'infuori di Allah, Egli è unico, non ha associati, a Lui appartiene la sovranità e a Lui sia lode, e Egli è capace di ogni cosa.
translations.ja: アッラーは唯一の神であり、他に類を見ない。彼は唯一であり、配偶者を持たない。主権は彼に属し、すべての賞賛は彼のものである。そして、彼は万事を成し遂げることができる。
translations.ko: 알라 외에 다른 신은 없으며, 그분은 유일하시며, 그분에게는 동반자가 없습니다. 주권은 그분께 속하며, 찬양은 그분께 드려집니다. 그리고 그분은 모든 것을 하실 수 있습니다.
translations.nl: Er is geen god behalve Allah, Hij is alleen, Hij heeft geen partner, Hem toebehoort de soevereiniteit en Hem toebehoort alle lof, en Hij is in staat alles te doen.
translations.pl: Nie ma boga poza Allah'em, On jest jedyny, nie ma u Niego partnera, Jemu należy zwierzchnictwo, a Jemu należy chwała, a On jest zdolny uczynić wszystko.
translations.pt-br: Não há divindade senão Allah, Ele é único, não tem parceiro, a Ele pertence a soberania e a Ele pertence a louvor, e Ele é capaz de todas as coisas.
translations.ru: Нет бога, кроме Аллаха. Он един, у Него нет сотоварища. Ему принадлежит власть, и Ему принадлежит вся хвала. Он всемогущ.
translations.sv: Det finns ingen gud förutom Allah, Han är unik, Han har ingen partner, till Honom tillhör all makt, och till Honom är all lovprisning, och Han är allsmäktig.
translations.th: ไม่มีพระเจ้าอื่นใดนอกจากอัลลอฮ์ พระองค์ทรงเอกเทศ พระองค์ไม่มีภาคีใดๆ อำนาจอธิปไตยเป็นของพระองค์เท่านั้น การสรรเสริญเป็นของพระองค์ และพระองค์ทรงสามารถทำสิ่งใดทุกสิ่ง
translations.tr:
لا إله إلا الله، وحده، لا شريك له، له الملك، وله الحمد، وهو على كل شيء قدير.

Türkçe Çevirisi: Allah'tan başka ilah yoktur, O tektir, O'nunla ortak yoktur, hükümranlık O'na aittir, övgü O'na mahsustur ve O, her şeye kâdirdir.
translations.uk: Немає бога, окрім Аллаха. Він єдиний, у Нього немає спільника. Йому належить вся влада, і Йому належить вся хвала, і Він всемогутній.
translations.vi: Không có vị thần nào ngoài Allah, Ngài là Đấng duy nhất, Ngài không có ai sánh ngang, quyền chủ tộcvà mọi sự tôn vinh đều thuộc về Ngài, và Ngài có khả năng làm mọi điều.

mantras.name: There is no god but Allah, He is alone, He has no partner; to Him belongs the sovereignty, and to Him belongs the praise, and He is able to do all things.
mantras.english: There is no god but Allah, He is alone, He has no partner; to Him belongs the sovereignty, and to Him belongs the praise, and He is able to do all things.
mantras.original: La ilaha illal-lah wahdahu la sharika lahu, lahul-mulk wa lahul-hamd wa huwa ala kulli shai’in qadir
mantras.transliteration: لا إله إلا الله، وحده لا شريك له; له الملك وله الحمد، وهو على كل شيء قدير.
mantras.background: From Islamic Dhikr tradition, this phrase means "No god but Allah, alone, no partner; His the sovereignty & praise; He is all-powerful." A timeless remembrance connecting hearts to the Divine.
mantras.benefits: Dhikr of "La ilaha illa Allah" brings profound peace, easing anxiety and uplifting mood. It deepens trust in Allah, sharpens focus, and connects the heart to divine presence—transforming daily life into a spiritual journey.
mantras.tags: islam, allah, dhikr, remembrance, tawakkul, supplication, faith, gratitude, peace, muslim, worship, devotion

translations.es: No hay más que Dios, Alá; Él es único, no tiene asociado; a Él pertenece la soberanía, y a Él pertenece la alabanza, y Él es capaz de hacer todas las cosas.
translations.ar: لا إله إلا الله، وحده لا شريك له؛ له الملك وله الحمد، وهو على كل شيء قدير.
translations.bn: আল্লাহ ছাড়া কোনো উপাস্য নেই, তিনি এক, তাঁর কোনো অংশীদার নেই; সার্বভৌমত্ব তাঁর, প্রশংসা তাঁর, এবং তিনি সবকিছু করতে সক্ষম।
translations.de: Es gibt keinen Gott außer Allah, Er ist einzigartig, Er hat keinen Partner; Ihm gehört die Souveränität, und Ihm gebührt aller Lob, und Er ist in der Lage, alles zu tun.
translations.en-us: There is no deity except Allah; He is alone, without any partner; to Him belongs all dominion, and to Him all praise is due, and He is capable of all things.
translations.en-gb: There is no deity worthy of worship save Allah; He is unique, without equal or partner; to Him belongs all dominion, and to Him all praise is due, and He is capable of all things.
translations.fr-fr: Il n'y a de divinité qu'Allah, Il est unique, Il n'a associé ; à Lui appartiennent la souveraineté et la louange, et Il est capable de toute chose.
translations.he: אין אלוהים אלא אללה, הוא לבדו, אין לו שותף; לו השליטה, ולו התהילה, והוא המסוגל לכל.
translations.hi: "अल्लाह के सिवा कोई ईश्वर नहीं है, वह अकेला है, उसका कोई साथी नहीं है; उसी के लिए संप्रभुता है, और उसी से स्तुति है, और वह सब कुछ करने में सक्षम है।"
translations.id: Tidak ada Tuhan kecuali Allah, Dia Maha Esa, Dia tidak memiliki sekutu; milik-Nya kedaulatan, dan hanya kepada-Nya segala puji; dan Dia mampu melakukan segala sesuatu.
translations.it: Non c'è altro dio all'infuori di Allah, Egli è unico, non ha associati; a Lui appartiene la sovranità, a Lui appartengono i lode, ed Egli è capace di fare ogni cosa.
translations.ja: 唯一の神はアッラーのみである。彼は唯一であり、配偶者はいない。主権は彼に属し、賛美も彼に帰する。そして、彼は万事を成し遂げることができる。
translations.ko: 알라 외에는 다른 신이 없으며, 그는 유일하며, 그에게는 동반자가 없습니다. 주권은 그에게 속하며, 찬양은 그에게 속하며, 그는 모든 것을 할 수 있습니다.
translations.nl: Er is geen god dan Allah, Hij is uniek, Hij heeft geen metgezel; Hem toebehoort de soevereiniteit, en Hem behoort de lof toe, en Hij is in staat alles te doen.
translations.pl: Nie ma boga poza Allah'em, On jest jedyny, nie ma u Niego partnera; Jemu należy władza, a Jemu chwała, a On jest zdolny do wszystkiego.
translations.pt-br: Não há divindade senão Allah, Ele é único, não tem parceiro; a Ele pertence a soberania, e a Ele pertence a louvor, e Ele é capaz de realizar todas as coisas.
translations.ru: Нет бога, кроме Аллаха. Он Един, у Него нет сотоварища; Ему принадлежит власть, и Ему хвала, и Он способен на всё.
translations.sv: Det finns ingen gud utom Allah, Han är unik, Han har ingen partner; Hans är herraväldet, och Hans är all lovprisning, och Han är allsmäktig.
translations.th: ไม่มีพระเจ้าอื่นใดนอกจากอัลลอฮ์ พระองค์ทรงเอกดั้ง พระองค์ไม่มีภาคีใดๆ อำนาจอธิปไตยเป็นของพระองค์ ความสรรเสริญเป็นของพระองค์ และพระองค์ทรงสามารถทำสิ่งใดๆ ได้
translations.tr:
لا إله إلا الله، وحده لا شريك له؛ له الملك، وله الحمد، وهو على كل شيء قدير.

Türkçe çevirisi: Allah'tan başka ilah yoktur, O'nun tek olduğu, hiçbir ortağının olmadığı; hükümranlık O'na aittir, övgü O'nadır ve O, her şeye kâdirdir.
translations.uk: Немає бога, окрім Аллаха, Він є єдиний, у Нього немає жодного партнера; Йому належить влада, і Йому належить хвала, і Він всемогутній.
translations.vi: Không có Thượng Đế nào khác ngoài Allah, Ngài là Đấng Một, Ngài không có ai sánh ngang; Quyền uy thuộc về Ngài, sự ca ngợi thuộc về Ngài, và Ngài có thể làm mọi điều.
translations.zh-cn: “除真主之外，别无神祇。他是独一的，没有伙伴；主权属于他，赞美归于他，他全能。”

mantras.name: There is no power and no strength except in Allah.
mantras.english: There is no power and no strength except in Allah.
mantras.original: La hawla wa la quwwata illa billah
mantras.transliteration: lā ḥawla wa lā quwwata ʾillā bi-llāh.
mantras.background: From Islamic dhikr tradition, "Lā ḥawla wa lā quwwata illa billāh" means "No power nor might except Allah." Called a Paradise treasure (Bukhari, Muslim), it transforms surrender into strength.
mantras.benefits: "La hawla wa la quwwata illa billah" cultivates surrender & resilience. Described as a treasure of Paradise (Bukhari, Muslim), it eases anxiety and redirects one's strength toward divine trust.
mantras.tags: islamic, dhikr, allah, reliance, trust, surrender, strength, protection, empower

translations.es: No hay poder ni fuerza, excepto en Alá.
translations.ar: ليس هناك قوة ولا سلطان إلا بالله.
translations.bn: আল্লাহ ছাড়া কোনো শক্তি এবং কোনো ক্ষমতা নেই।
translations.de: Es gibt keine Macht und keine Stärke außer bei Allah.
translations.en-us: There is no power and no strength except from Allah.
translations.en-gb: There is no power and no strength except in Allah.
translations.fr-fr: Il n'y a de force ni de puissance qu'en Dieu.
translations.he: אין כוח ולא עוצמה אלא באללה.
translations.hi: "अल्लाह के सिवा कोई शक्ति और कोई ताक़त नहीं है।"
translations.id: Tidak ada kekuatan dan tidak ada kemampuan kecuali dari Allah.
translations.it: Non c'è potere né forza se non in Allah.
translations.ja: 力も、強さも、アッラーのみにのみ存在する。
translations.ko: 힘과 능력은 오직 알라께로부터 옵니다. (Him-gwa neungnyeok-eun ojik Allah-kke-boudeom-ni-da.)
translations.nl: Er is geen macht en geen kracht behalve in Allah.
translations.pl: Nie ma władzy ani siły poza Allem.
translations.pt-br: Não há poder nem força senão em Alá.
translations.ru: Нет силы и могущества, кроме как у Аллаха.
translations.sv: Det finns ingen makt och ingen styrka förutom från Allah.
translations.th: ไม่มีอำนาจและไม่มีความแข็งแกร่งใดๆ นอกจากจากพระเจ้า (ไม่มีอำนาจและไม่มีความแข็งแกร่งใดๆ นอกเหนือจากพระเจ้า).
translations.tr: "Allah'tan başka ne güç ne de kuvvet vardır."
translations.uk: Немає сили і могутності, окрім Аллаха.
translations.vi: Không có quyền năng và không có sức mạnh nào ngoài Allah.
translations.zh-cn: 没有任何力量和能力，除非是源自真主 (Méiyǒu rènhé lìliàng hé nénglì, chúfēi shì yuányízhuānzhǔ).

mantras.name: Thus, it is so." or "Therefore, it is established.
mantras.english: Thus, it is so." or "Therefore, it is established.
mantras.transliteration: དེ་ལྟ་ཡིན་པས། (De lta yin bzhin.) or དེ་ལྟ་ནའི། (De lta na'i.)
mantras.background: From Islam's Sufi mystics, "Hū" (هُو) means simply "He" — yet points beyond all names to God's pure Essence. The whirling dervishes exhale it to dissolve the ego.
mantras.benefits: Chanting Hū dissolves ego boundaries, opening the heart to pure divine presence. Sufi masters link it to deep chest resonance, vagus nerve activation, and gradual inner transformation — until even sleep becomes remembrance.
mantras.tags: islamic, sufi, dhikr, mevlevi, rumi, transformation, liberation, unity, divine-presence, essence, breath-work

translations.es: Por lo tanto, está establecido." or "Así es, de hecho."
translations.ar: "ولهذا، فقد ثبت الأمر." or "لذلك، فقد أُثبت ذلك."
translations.bn: অতএব, এটি সত্য।" অথবা, "অতএব, এটি প্রমাণিত।"
translations.de: Somit ist es der Fall." oder "Folglich ist es bewiesen/bestätigt."
translations.en-us: "Therefore, it is so." or "Consequently, it is established."
translations.en-gb: "Therefore, it is so." or "Consequently, it is confirmed."
translations.fr-fr: Ainsi, c'est le cas." or "Par conséquent, cela est établi."
translations.he: לפיכך, זהו המצב. / לכן, זה הוכח.
translations.hi: इस प्रकार, यह सत्य है।" या "इसलिए, यह सिद्ध हुआ।"
translations.id: Maka, demikianlah adanya." atau "Oleh karena itu, hal itu telah ditegaskan."
translations.it: Pertanto, è così." oppure "Di conseguenza, è stabilito."
translations.ja: したがって、それは真実です。(したがって、それが確立されました。)
translations.ko: 그러므로, 그것은 사실입니다. 또는, 따라서, 그것은 확정되었습니다.
translations.nl: Daarom is het zo." of "Daarom is het vastgesteld."
translations.pl: Zatem, tak jest." lub "W związku z tym, zostało to potwierdzone."
translations.pt-br: Assim, é assim que é." ou "Portanto, está estabelecido."
translations.ru: Таким образом, это установлено." или "Следовательно, это подтверждается."
translations.sv: Därför är det så." eller "Därmed är det fastställt."
translations.th: ดังนั้น, มันจึงเป็นเช่นนั้น หรือ ดังนั้น, มันได้รับการยืนยันแล้ว
translations.tr: Bu nedenle, bu böyledir." veya "Bu nedenle, bu kesinleşmiştir."
translations.uk: Таким чином, це встановлено." або "Отже, це доведено."
translations.vi: Vậy, điều đó là như vậy." hoặc "Do đó, điều đó đã được chứng minh."
translations.zh-cn: 因此，这就是事实。 或者，因此，这是确定的。

mantras.name: Verily, it is through the remembrance of Allah that hearts find peace.
mantras.english: Verily, it is through the remembrance of Allah that hearts find peace.
mantras.transliteration: إِنَّمَا السَّلاَمُ لِلنُّفُوسِ بِذِكْرِ ٱللَّٰهِ.
mantras.background: From the Quran (Surah Ar-Ra'd), this Arabic phrase — "Alā bidhikri llāhi taṭmaʾinnu l-qulūb" — means "in the remembrance of Allah, hearts find peace," rooted in Islam's earliest prophetic traditions.
mantras.benefits: Dhikr calms anxiety, builds emotional resilience, and deepens divine connection. Its repetitive remembrance stills the restless mind, offering tranquility rooted in centuries of Islamic spiritual tradition.
mantras.tags: islamic, dhikr, allah, remembrance, peace, tranquility, sufi, heart, mindfulness, resilience

translations.es: En verdad, es a través del recuerdo de Allah que los corazones encuentran la paz.
translations.ar: إنَّ الذِّكرَ للهِ هو الذي يجدُ بهِ القلوبُ السِّلامَ.
translations.bn: নিঃসন্দেহে, পরম করুণাময় আল্লাহকে স্মরণ করার মাধ্যমেই হৃদয় শান্তি খুঁজে পায়।
translations.de: Wahrlich, durch die Erinnerung an Allah finden die Herzen Ruhe.
translations.en-us: Indeed, it is through the remembrance of Allah that hearts attain tranquility.
translations.en-gb: Indeed, it is through the remembrance of God that hearts attain serenity.
translations.fr-fr: En vérité, c'est par le souvenir d'Allah que les cœurs trouvent la paix.
translations.he: אכן, בזכירת אללה, הלבבות מוצאים שלווה.
translations.hi: निस्संदेह, यह अल्लाह की याद के माध्यम से ही है कि दिल शांति पाते हैं।
translations.id: Sesungguhnya, ketenangan hati dapat ditemukan melalui mengingat Allah.
translations.it: In verità, è attraverso il ricordo di Allah che i cuori trovano pace.
translations.ja: 確かに、心の平安は、アッラーを思い出すことによって得られます。
translations.ko: 진실로, 알라를 기억함으로써 마음은 평화를 찾습니다. (Jinsilro, Allah-reul gieokbeusimyeon, maeumeun pyeonghwaleul chatseumnida.)
translations.nl: Waarlijk, het is door de herinnering aan Allah dat harten vrede vinden.
translations.pl: Bez wątpienia, to poprzez wspomnienie Allaha serca znajdują ukojenie.
translations.pt-br: Em verdade, é através da lembrança de Allah que os corações encontram a paz.
translations.ru: Воистину, именно посредством памяти о Аллахе сердца обретают спокойствие.
translations.sv: Sannerligen, det är genom att minnas Allah som hjärtan finner frid.
translations.th: แท้จริงแล้ว ความสงบสุขของจิตใจย่อมเกิดขึ้นได้จากการรำลึกถึงพระเจ้า.
translations.tr: Şüphesiz ki, Allah'ı anmak, kalplere huzur getirir.
translations.uk: Ось, справді, саме через згадку про Аллаха серця знаходять спокій.
translations.vi: Thực sự, chỉ bằng cách luôn ghi nhớ Allah, trái tim mới tìm được sự bình yên.
translations.zh-cn: 确实，通过对真主的纪念，人们的心灵才能获得安宁。

mantras.name: Ya Hayyu Ya Qayyum
mantras.english: O Ever-Living, O Self-Sustaining
mantras.transliteration: Yā Ḥayyu Yā Qayyūm
mantras.abstract:
Ya Hayyu Ya Qayyum invokes two of the divine names considered among the most exalted: Al-Hayy (the Ever-Living, eternally alive without beginning or end) and Al-Qayyum (the Self-Sustaining, who sustains all existence without depending on anything). These two names together appear in the Quran in Ayat al-Kursi (2:255), considered the greatest verse of the Quran.

The Prophet Muhammad is reported to have said: 'Seek assistance through Ya Hayyu Ya Qayyum.' It is specifically recommended in states of anxiety, illness, fatigue, or spiritual dryness — conditions where the practitioner feels their own life-force is depleted. By invoking the source of all life and sustenance, they access a reservoir beyond their own diminished resources.

In the science of Islamic divine names (ilm al-asma), Al-Hayy and Al-Qayyum represent the poles of divine immanence and transcendence respectively. Al-Hayy is the living pulse that animates every creature from within; Al-Qayyum is the transcendent support without which nothing could maintain its existence for an instant. Invoking them together is a complete metaphysical invocation.

Sufi teachers, particularly in the Naqshbandi tradition, use Ya Hayyu Ya Qayyum as a specific heart-opening practice. The heart is understood to have its own form of life distinct from biological heartbeat — a spiritual vitality that can wither under negligence and worldliness. Practitioners report a sensation of warmth and expansion in the chest that signals renewed spiritual life.

Physical healing traditions within Islamic medicine also associate these names with specific physiological effects. Ya Hayyu is associated with vitality and immune function; Ya Qayyum with structural integrity and stability. Many practitioners recite them during illness, directing the vibration of the names to affected areas of the body.

translations.en: O Ever-Living, O Self-Sustaining
translations.zh: 啊，永生者，啊，自养者
translations.es: ¡Oh Eterno Viviente! ¡Oh Sustentador de todo!

mantras.name: Ya Rahman Ya Rahim
mantras.english: O Most Gracious, O Most Merciful
mantras.transliteration: Yā Raḥmānu Yā Raḥīm
mantras.abstract:
Ya Rahman Ya Rahim invokes the two supreme attributes of divine mercy that frame the entire Quran, appearing in the Bismillah at the opening of 113 chapters. Rahman (the Most Gracious) denotes the all-encompassing mercy that includes all beings regardless of their faith or conduct — it is cosmic, unconditional, and identical with existence itself. Rahim (the Most Merciful) denotes the specific mercy reserved for the believers — intimate, responsive, and increasingly present as the heart opens.

The distinction between Rahman and Rahim is subtle but important in Islamic spirituality. Rahman is said to be the mercy that creates — it is why anything exists at all rather than nothing. Rahim is the mercy that sustains, guides, and ultimately returns the soul to its origin. Together, invoking them places the practitioner consciously within this field of care at both the cosmic and personal level.

In Sufi practice, these two names are used as a healing invocation. Teachers prescribe Ya Rahman for conditions related to hardheartedness or disconnection from the beauty of existence — the cosmic mercy of Rahman is said to open perception to the goodness woven into creation. Ya Rahim is used for grief, loss, and longing — the intimate mercy of Rahim draws the suffering heart into the embrace of divine care.

Rumi's poem 'The Guest House' — welcoming every emotion as a divine messenger — is an extended meditation on Rahman and Rahim. The mercy of God arrives in unexpected forms: through joy, grief, difficulty, and grace alike. By addressing God as Rahman and Rahim, the practitioner trains the heart to recognize mercy even in unwelcome appearances.

The practice of repeating Ya Rahman Ya Rahim 100 times after Fajr (dawn) prayer is recommended by many teachers as a way of beginning the day from a ground of divine care rather than anxiety or obligation. Practitioners report that days begun with this invocation have a different quality — not that circumstances improve necessarily, but that the inner response shifts from contraction to openness.

translations.en: O Most Gracious, O Most Merciful
translations.zh: 啊，至仁的，啊，至慈的
translations.es: ¡Oh el Compasivo! ¡Oh el Misericordioso!

mantras.name: Ahimsa Paramo Dharma
mantras.english: Non-violence is the highest dharma — the supreme ethical principle
mantras.transliteration: Ahiṃsā paramo dharmaḥ
mantras.abstract:
Ahimsa Paramo Dharma — Non-violence is the Highest Dharma (righteousness/truth) — is the foundational ethical declaration of Jainism and one of the most important moral principles in Indian philosophy. Non-violence (ahimsa) is not merely one virtue among many in Jainism but the supreme virtue from which all others derive. The Jain ethical system holds that the avoidance of harm to any living being — from the most microscopic to the most complex — is the primary obligation of a conscious being.

Mahavira taught that every living being (jiva) possesses a soul with infinite consciousness, infinite bliss, infinite knowledge, and infinite energy — these capacities being obscured to varying degrees by accumulated karma. To harm any living being is therefore to harm a soul that shares the same fundamental nature as one's own. The Golden Rule of Jainism is thus rooted not in empathy (imagining what another feels) but in identity (recognizing that the other's soul is ontologically equal to yours).

The practice of ahimsa in Jainism extends far beyond the obvious prohibitions against physical violence. Jain monks and nuns sweep the ground before them to avoid stepping on insects, strain their water to avoid swallowing microorganisms, and wear masks over their mouths to prevent accidentally inhaling small creatures. This is not obsessive-compulsive behavior but the logical extension of a completely consistent commitment to non-harm: if all souls are equal, then the soul in a bacterium deserves the same consideration as the soul in a human being.

For lay Jains, ahimsa shapes diet (vegetarianism, often veganism), profession (avoiding industries that cause large-scale harm), speech (avoiding cruel or manipulative language), and thought (attempting to cultivate compassion toward all beings, including those one dislikes). The practice of ahimsa is thus a total training of consciousness — a continuous, twenty-four-hour practice of expanding the circle of one's compassion.

As a mantra, Ahimsa Paramo Dharma is recited as a commitment statement — an affirmation of one's intention to live by the highest principle. When facing a decision that involves potential harm — to another person, to an animal, to the environment — pausing to whisper these words re-centers the practitioner on their deepest values. Many Jain teachers prescribe reciting it upon waking as the first intentional statement of the day: before any other activity, one affirms that non-violence is the governing principle of all that follows.

translations.en: Non-violence is the highest dharma
translations.zh: 非暴力是最高的法——最高的伦理原则
translations.es: La no violencia es el dharma supremo

mantras.name: Anekantavada
mantras.english: Many-sidedness — no single viewpoint captures the whole truth
mantras.transliteration: Anekāntavāda
mantras.abstract:
Anekantavada — the doctrine of many-sidedness (literally 'non-one-endedness') — is one of the most distinctive and philosophically sophisticated contributions of Jainism to world thought. It holds that ultimate reality (absolute truth) is infinitely complex and cannot be completely captured by any single viewpoint, statement, or perspective. All partial views contain some truth; no partial view contains all truth. This is not relativism (the idea that all views are equally correct) but epistemic humility (the recognition that any statement can be at most partially true).

Anekantavada is expressed practically through two corollary doctrines: Syadvada (the theory of conditional predication — every statement should be prefaced with 'maybe' or 'in some respect') and Nayavada (the theory of partial viewpoints — each legitimate viewpoint illuminates one aspect of reality). Together these produce a philosophy of radical intellectual tolerance: no one tradition, scripture, teacher, or philosophical system can claim to have captured the whole truth, and the wise person holds their views lightly while remaining open to what others' perspectives might illuminate.

The practical implications of Anekantavada for spiritual life are profound. If no single viewpoint is complete, then spiritual arrogance — the certainty that one's own path is the only true path — is, in the Jain framework, a form of ignorance. The Jain sage respects all genuine spiritual paths as partial illuminations of the infinite truth, without abandoning the specific Jain path which they experience as the most complete available illumination.

As a mantra and contemplative practice, Anekantavada is used as a softening agent for fixed views. When one notices oneself becoming certain, judgmental, or dismissive of another's perspective, recalling Anekantavada — 'reality is many-sided' — invites a pause and a genuine inquiry: what might be true in this other viewpoint that I am currently unable to see? This practice cultivates the quality of intellectual non-violence (ahimsa of the mind) that Jainism considers as important as physical non-violence.

In the contemporary world of polarization and ideological conflict, Anekantavada offers a philosophical framework for genuine dialogue. Neither relativism (all views are equal, so it doesn't matter what you believe) nor dogmatism (my view is correct, yours is wrong), it offers a third path: charitable attention to the partial truth in all genuine perspectives, combined with the ongoing recognition that reality itself exceeds any of our descriptions of it.

translations.en: Many-sidedness — no single viewpoint captures the whole truth
translations.zh: 多面性——没有单一观点能捕捉全部真理
translations.es: Multiplicidad de perspectivas — ningún punto de vista único capta toda la verdad

mantras.name: Jai Jinendra
mantras.english: Victory to the Jina — honor to the conquerors of the self
mantras.transliteration: Jai Jinendra
mantras.abstract:
Jai Jinendra — Victory to the Jinendra (Lord of the Jinas/Conquerors) — is the traditional Jain greeting, equivalent to 'Namaste' in Hindu culture or 'Sat Sri Akal' in Sikh culture. When two Jains meet, they exchange Jai Jinendra as a mutual acknowledgment of the highest spiritual aspiration: that both parties are, in their deepest nature, on the path of the Jina — the conqueror of the passions and the liberator of the soul.

The word 'Jina' (Conqueror) gives Jainism its name. A Jina is not a warrior who conquers external enemies but a hero of the inner life who has conquered the inner foes: attachment (raga), aversion (dvesha), delusion (moha), anger (krodha), pride (mana), deceit (maya), and greed (lobha). The 24 Tirthankaras (ford-makers) of the current cosmic age, of whom Mahavira was the most recent, are the supreme examples of this inner conquest.

By greeting each other with Jai Jinendra, Jains are both celebrating the example of the Tirthankaras and implicitly committing to the path of inner conquest themselves. The greeting is simultaneously backward-looking (honoring those who have already achieved liberation) and forward-looking (affirming the possibility and intention of liberation in oneself). This dual orientation — gratitude to those who have shown the way, commitment to walk it oneself — is the essence of Jain spiritual culture.

The practice of Jai Jinendra as a mantra involves holding in awareness the image and presence of the Jina — the fully liberated being whose consciousness is described as omniscient (sarvajnya), all-seeing, and completely free from all karmic matter. By turning attention toward this image of perfect liberation, the practitioner activates the latent capacity for liberation in their own consciousness. In Jain metaphysics, the fully liberated soul (siddha) and the unliberated soul (sansari) share the same fundamental nature; what differs is only the degree of karmic obscuration.

As a daily practice, Jai Jinendra can be whispered as an aspiration upon waking — 'Victory to the Jina' meaning 'may I today move in the direction of inner victory over my passions.' It can be repeated during moments of temptation, anger, or greed as a reminder of the highest standard to which one aspires. The word 'jai' (victory) carries the energy of celebration and encouragement, making this mantra one of Jainism's most joyful.

translations.en: Victory to the Jina — honor to the conquerors of the self
translations.zh: 耆那胜利——向自我征服者致敬
translations.es: Victoria al Jina — honor a los conquistadores del ser

mantras.name: Jai Mahavir
mantras.english: Victory to Mahavira — honor to the great hero of the current age
mantras.transliteration: Jai Mahāvīr
mantras.abstract:
Jai Mahavir — Victory to Mahavira — is the devotional salutation to Vardhamana Mahavira (599-527 BCE), the 24th Tirthankara (ford-maker, one who creates the crossing from samsara to liberation) of the current cosmic cycle and the revitalizer of the Jain tradition. Mahavira, whose name means 'Great Hero,' is not celebrated as a god who can grant boons but as the supreme exemplar of a human being who achieved liberation through the most rigorous inner discipline and can therefore show others the way.

Mahavira's life story is a testament to the Jain path taken to its most extreme expression. After renouncing royal wealth at the age of 30, he practiced severe austerity for 12 years and 6 months — including extended periods of complete motionless standing meditation in difficult conditions, total silence, and fasting. At the age of 42, he attained kevala jnana (omniscience) under a Sal tree and spent the remaining 30 years teaching the path of liberation to thousands of monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen.

The salutation Jai Mahavir is an act of alignment with the qualities Mahavira embodies: extraordinary inner strength (mahavira literally means 'great hero'), complete detachment from sensory experience, fearlessness before death, compassion for all beings, and the total transparency of a soul that has removed every veil between itself and infinite knowing. By invoking his victory, the devotee implicitly aspires to the same inner conquest.

In Jain iconography, Mahavira is depicted in the standing posture of complete equipoise (kayotsarga) — arms hanging freely at the sides, eyes open and unfocused, body perfectly still. This image represents the state of total non-attachment in which the soul is fully aware but completely undisturbed. Meditating on this image while reciting Jai Mahavir invites the practitioner to touch, however briefly, the quality of imperturbable inner stillness that Mahavira permanently attained.

Jain festivals associated with Mahavira — particularly Mahavir Jayanti (his birth celebration) — are occasions for communal recitation of Jai Mahavir, processions, charitable giving, and the renewal of spiritual commitments. The collective celebration of Mahavira's example reinforces the Jain community's shared aspiration and reminds each individual that the path of liberation, however difficult, has been walked successfully by at least one human being.

translations.en: Victory to Mahavira — honor to the great hero of the current age
translations.zh: 大雄胜利——向当世大英雄致敬
translations.es: Victoria a Mahavira — honor al gran héroe de la era actual

mantras.name: Micchami Dukkadam
mantras.english: May all my wrongdoings be forgiven — the Jain formula of universal forgiveness
mantras.transliteration: Micchāmi Dukkadaṃ
mantras.abstract:
Micchami Dukkadam — May all my wrongdoings be forgiven — is the Jain formula of universal forgiveness and apology, traditionally exchanged between Jains at the conclusion of the sacred festival of Paryushana (annual festival of forgiveness) and the following day (Samvatsari). On this occasion, Jains ask forgiveness from every being — present or absent, known or unknown — for any harm done during the past year through thought, word, or deed.

The practice is remarkable in its comprehensiveness. One does not merely ask forgiveness from those one has obviously wronged; one asks forgiveness from all beings in all directions, known and unknown, intentionally or unintentionally harmed. The formula acknowledges the Jain understanding that even the most careful person inevitably causes some harm simply by existing — by breathing, by eating, by moving. The annual forgiveness ritual is an acknowledgment of this unavoidable reality and a renewal of the commitment to minimize harm going forward.

The exchange of Micchami Dukkadam between Jains on Samvatsari day is a powerful social practice that has maintained communal harmony across centuries. By asking and granting forgiveness simultaneously — both parties say the phrase to each other — the ritual prevents the accumulation of grievances and resentments that corrode community life. Whatever happened in the past year is forgiven; the relationship is renewed; the new year begins clean.

In Jain metaphysics, forgiveness has specific karmic implications: unforgiven resentment creates a karmic bond between the person who holds it and the one they resent, perpetuating entanglement across lifetimes. By granting sincere forgiveness, one severs this karmic thread and frees both parties. The practice of Micchami Dukkadam is thus not merely ethical but cosmological — a technology of karmic liberation.

As a daily practice, Micchami Dukkadam can be recited each evening as a review of the day. Before sleep, the practitioner mentally asks forgiveness from any being they may have harmed during the day — through irritability, carelessness, harsh words, or neglect. This brief practice prevents the accumulation of karmic residue and maintains the clarity of conscience that Jains call shuddha bhava (pure disposition).

translations.en: May all my wrongdoings be forgiven
translations.zh: 愿我所有的过失得到宽恕
translations.es: Que todas mis acciones incorrectas sean perdonadas

mantras.name: Namo Arihantanam
mantras.english: Homage to the Arihantas — the enlightened beings still in the world
mantras.transliteration: Ṇamo Arihantāṇaṃ
mantras.abstract:
Namo Arihantanam — Homage to the Arihantas — is the opening line of the Navkar Mantra and a complete mantra in its own right. The Arihantas (also spelled Arihants or Arhats) are souls who have attained complete omniscience (kevala jnana) while still embodied — they have destroyed all four destructive karmas (ghatia karma) and now exist in a state of perfect knowledge, perfect perception, perfect bliss, and perfect power, while still temporarily maintaining a physical body.

The Arihantas are unique in Jain cosmology because they are simultaneously the highest spiritual ideal and the most accessible: they are still in the world, they still teach, they are still available to be seen and heard by others. The 24 Tirthankaras of the current cosmic cycle — among whom Mahavira (599-527 BCE) was the most recent — were Arihantas during their earthly lives. By saluting the Arihantas, one salutes both the highest possible human attainment and the living presence of that attainment in the world.

The specific quality celebrated in the Arihanta is the conquest of the four passions (kashayas): anger, pride, deceit, and greed. These are the inner enemies that keep the soul bound to the cycle of rebirth (samsara). An Arihanta has destroyed them completely, not merely suppressed them. This destruction, rather than suppression, is the hallmark of genuine liberation in Jainism — the radical elimination of the causes of suffering rather than their management.

In Jain puja (worship), Namo Arihantanam is recited while making offerings (typically flowers, water, incense, and other pure substances) to images of the Tirthankaras. The images, in the distinctive meditation posture (kayotsarga — body-abandonment), represent the state of complete inner stillness and detachment that the Arihanta has attained. The act of worship is an act of aspiration: by contemplating and honoring the Arihanta's perfection, the practitioner draws that perfection toward themselves.

As a standalone mantra, Namo Arihantanam is particularly powerful at moments when one is struggling with the inner passions — when anger arises, when pride inflates, when desire pulls strongly. By turning attention to the Arihanta who has conquered these very forces, the practitioner accesses both inspiration (this conquest is possible) and a cooling energy (the vibration of the mantra is associated with sattvic clarity) that can help dissolve the passion's grip.

translations.en: Homage to the Arihantas
translations.zh: 礼敬阿利汉塔——向仍在世间的觉悟众生致敬
translations.es: Homenaje a los Arihantas

mantras.name: Namo Siddhanam
mantras.english: Homage to the Siddhas — the liberated souls beyond all worlds
mantras.transliteration: Ṇamo Siddhāṇaṃ
mantras.abstract:
Namo Siddhanam — Homage to the Siddhas — is the second line of the Navkar Mantra and perhaps the most metaphysically profound. The Siddhas are souls who have attained complete liberation (moksha), shed all karmic matter — both destructive and non-destructive — and now reside in Siddhashila (the zone of the liberated at the apex of the cosmos) in a state of pure, infinite, permanent consciousness. They are beyond all worlds, beyond all births and deaths, beyond all interaction with the material universe.

The Siddhas represent the Jain ultimate: pure jiva (soul) in its original, unobscured state, experiencing infinite knowledge (ananta jnana), infinite perception (ananta darshana), infinite bliss (ananta sukha), and infinite energy (ananta virya). Unlike other liberation concepts where the liberated soul merges into an impersonal absolute, the Jain Siddha retains its individuality — each Siddha is a discrete unit of pure consciousness, distinct from all others, yet all sharing the same quality of infinite, blissful awareness.

The paradox of saluting the Siddhas is that they cannot hear prayers, respond to worship, or grant blessings — they are completely beyond all interaction with the manifest world. Jain philosophers are quite clear on this point. And yet Namo Siddhanam is among the most powerful mantras in Jainism. The explanation: by directing consciousness toward the quality of perfect liberation, the practitioner activates and resonates with that quality latent within their own soul. The salutation is, in a sense, a salutation to one's own highest potential.

In Jain meditation, contemplation of the Siddhas involves the practice of shuddhatma bhavana — meditating on one's own soul as it truly is, stripped of all karmic obscuration, in its pure state of infinite awareness. The practitioner temporarily suspends identification with the body, emotions, and thoughts, and rests in the recognition of pure consciousness. 'I am not the body, not the mind, not the karma — I am the pure jiva, of the nature of the Siddha.' This is the highest form of Jain meditation.

Namo Siddhanam resonates with the highest teachings of non-dual traditions across cultures. The Advaita Vedanta recognition 'I am Brahman,' the Buddhist recognition 'You are Buddha-nature,' and the Jain recognition 'My soul is Siddha by nature' all point to the same discovery: liberation is not a future achievement but a present reality obscured by conditioned overlays. Namo Siddhanam is the Jain way of pointing to and celebrating this fundamental truth.

translations.en: Homage to the Siddhas — the liberated souls beyond all worlds
translations.zh: 礼敬悉达——超越一切世界的解脱灵魂
translations.es: Homenaje a los Siddhas — las almas liberadas más allá de todos los mundos

mantras.name: Navkar Mantra
mantras.english: Homage to the Five Supreme Beings — the universal Jain salutation
mantras.original: णमो अरिहंताणं णमो सिद्धाणं णमो आयरियाणं णमो उवज्झायाणं णमो लोए सव्व-साहूणं
mantras.transliteration: Ṇamo Arihantāṇaṃ Ṇamo Siddhāṇaṃ Ṇamo Āyariyāṇaṃ Ṇamo Uvajjhāyāṇaṃ Ṇamo Loe Savva-Sāhūṇaṃ
mantras.abstract:
The Navkar Mantra — also called the Namokar Mantra, Panca Namaskara, or Panch Parmeshthi Namaskara — is the most sacred and fundamental prayer in Jainism, and is considered the greatest mantra in the Jain universe. It consists of salutations (namaskara) to five categories of supreme beings (panca parameshthi): the Arihantas (enlightened beings still in a body), the Siddhas (liberated souls), the Acharyas (spiritual heads of the monastic order), the Upadhyayas (teachers of Jain scripture), and all the Sadhus (Jain monks and nuns).

Uniquely among the world's great mantras, the Navkar Mantra does not address any particular deity or God by name. It is a salutation to the quality of liberation and the path that leads to it — wherever those qualities are found. By bowing to the Arihantas rather than to a specific person, the mantra acknowledges that the quality of enlightenment is a universal possibility, not the exclusive property of any individual or historical figure. One bows to what they have attained, not merely to who they were.

The mantra is traditionally recited 108 times with a rosary (mala) as a complete session of meditation. It can also be recited as a single salutation before meals, before sleep, upon waking, before any important undertaking, or as a constant accompaniment to breath throughout the day. Jain teachers say that even hearing the Navkar Mantra recited sincerely — without understanding a single word — is sufficient to create positive karmic impressions (samskara) in the consciousness.

In Jain cosmology, the five categories of supreme beings represent the stages of spiritual perfection: the Arihantas are still in the world and can be approached; the Siddhas have dissolved all karmic matter and are beyond physical form; the Acharyas, Upadhyayas, and Sadhus represent the living path in the current era. By saluting all five, the practitioner aligns with the entire spectrum of the liberation path — from the perfect teachers still accessible in this world to the pure souls beyond all worlds.

The Navkar Mantra is said to be so powerful that it can mitigate the effects of accumulated negative karma, provide protection in dangerous circumstances, and create the conditions for moksha (liberation). More immediately, regular recitation is reported to produce a profound inner calm — what Jains call samata (equanimity) — which is both the means and the foretaste of liberation itself.

translations.en: Homage to the Arihantas, the Siddhas, the Acharyas, the Upadhyayas, and all the Sadhus in the universe
translations.zh: 礼敬阿利汉塔、悉达、阿查里亚、乌帕贾亚以及宇宙中所有的萨杜
translations.es: Homenaje a los Arihantas, los Siddhas, los Acharyas, los Upadhyayas y todos los Sadhus del universo

mantras.name: Om Namo Arhat Siddhacharyopadhyaya Sarva Sadhu Bhyo
mantras.english: Om, salutation to the Arhats, Siddhas, Acharyas, Upadhyayas, and all Sadhus
mantras.original: ॐ नमो अर्हत् सिद्धाचार्योपाध्याय सर्व साधुभ्यो
mantras.transliteration: Oṃ namo arhat siddhācāryopādhyāya sarva sādhubhyo
mantras.abstract:
This Sanskrit rendering of the Navkar Mantra opens with the sacred syllable Om and salutes all five categories of supreme beings in a single extended salutation. While the Prakrit Navkar Mantra is the canonical form for Digambara and Shvetambara Jains, this Sanskrit version is used in many Jain scriptural recitations and scholarly contexts, and serves as a bridge between Jain teachings and the broader Sanskrit philosophical tradition.

The inclusion of Om at the beginning of this form is significant. Om is not traditionally a Jain syllable — Jainism generally rejects the Vedic framework within which Om operates — but in its universal application as the primordial sound current, Om has been accepted in many Jain traditions as a prefix that resonates with the vibrational reality the Navkar Mantra addresses. Some Jain commentators identify Om as an abbreviation of the five salutations themselves: O = Arihanta, U = Upadhyaya, and M = Muni (Sadhu), with the nasal resonance (anusvara) representing the Siddha and Acharya together.

The compressed syntax of this mantra — all five salutations in one breath — creates a sense of encompassing totality. The practitioner bows to the entire spiritual hierarchy in a single gesture, recognizing the interconnected spiritual ecosystem that sustains the path of liberation. No level of that hierarchy is more important than another; all are honoured equally in a single comprehensive salutation.

This form of the Navkar Mantra is particularly useful in interfaith contexts and in communities where the Prakrit form may be unfamiliar. Its Sanskrit renders it accessible to practitioners trained in Vedic or Buddhist Sanskrit mantras, while preserving the core Jain theological content. Many contemporary Jain teachers use this form in yoga and meditation settings where participants come from diverse backgrounds.

As a meditation practice, this extended salutation can be recited slowly with a brief pause after each of the five categories — Arhat, Siddha, Acharya, Upadhyaya, Sadhu — with each pause used to contemplate the specific quality of spiritual attainment represented by each category. This five-fold contemplation is a complete survey of the Jain path from the aspiring practitioner (sadhu) through the living teacher (acharya/upadhyaya) to the perfected being (arhat) and ultimately the liberated soul (siddha).

translations.en: Om, salutation to the Arhats, Siddhas, Acharyas, Upadhyayas, and all Sadhus
translations.zh: 唵，礼敬阿利汉特、悉达、阿查里亚、乌帕贾亚和所有萨杜
translations.es: Om, salutación a los Arhats, Siddhas, Acharyas, Upadhyayas y todos los Sadhus

mantras.name: Parasparopagraho Jivanam
mantras.english: Living beings render service to one another — the law of interdependence
mantras.transliteration: Parasparopaghraho Jīvānām
mantras.abstract:
Parasparopagraho Jivanam — 'Living beings render service to one another' — is one of the most important philosophical statements in Jain scripture, found in the Tattvarthasutra of Umasvati (the closest Jainism has to a systematic philosophical text). It articulates the Jain understanding of interdependence: all beings exist in a web of mutual support, and the flourishing of each depends on the consideration given to all others.

This principle stands in complementary relationship to the Jain doctrine of independent souls (jivatva). While each soul is ultimately responsible for its own liberation, the practical reality of existence in the world is one of radical interdependence. The air I breathe was produced by plants; the food I eat grew in soil enriched by countless organisms; the knowledge I have came from teachers. To recognize this interdependence is to understand why ahimsa is not just an ethical obligation but a cosmological reality: in harming others, one harms the web that supports oneself.

In modern ecological thought, this principle finds an obvious resonance with the concept of ecosystems and the interdependence of species. Jains are fond of pointing out that Parasparopagraho Jivanam is an ancient recognition of what ecology confirms: all beings are knitted into networks of mutual service, and the destruction of any part degrades the whole. Jain environmental ethics, grounded in this principle, is one of the world's oldest ecological philosophies.

As a mantra and contemplation, Parasparopagraho Jivanam invites the practitioner to trace the networks of interdependence that sustain their daily life. Who grew this food? Who made this clothing? What air am I breathing and where did it come from? What water am I drinking? The contemplation of these questions gradually dissolves the illusion of self-sufficiency and produces a quality of natural gratitude and care toward all the beings whose service makes one's life possible.

In Jain meditation practice, this phrase is used as an antidote to the competitive mindset — the belief that success comes at the expense of others. By holding Parasparopagraho Jivanam in awareness, the practitioner cultivates a different basic orientation: life is not a competition but a collaboration. The universe's default mode is mutual support. Human striving against this principle is the source of much unnecessary suffering.

translations.en: Living beings render service to one another
translations.zh: 众生相互服务——相互依存的法则
translations.es: Los seres vivos se prestan servicio mutuamente

mantras.name: Samyak Darshana Jnana Charitra
mantras.english: Right Vision, Right Knowledge, Right Conduct — the Jain threefold path
mantras.transliteration: Samyak Darśana Jñāna Cāritra
mantras.abstract:
Samyak Darshana Jnana Charitra — Right Vision, Right Knowledge, Right Conduct — is the threefold path of Jain liberation (moksha marga), the complete description of what is required for the soul to free itself from karmic bondage. Samyak Darshana (right faith/vision) is the correct understanding of the nature of the soul and reality; Samyak Jnana (right knowledge) is accurate knowledge of the seven fundamental truths (tattvas) of Jainism; and Samyak Charitra (right conduct) is living in accordance with the ethical implications of that vision and knowledge.

The sequence is significant: vision before knowledge before conduct. One must first have the correct orientation — the inner recognition that the soul is the true self and liberation is possible — before any amount of scriptural knowledge or ethical striving can be genuinely liberating. Right vision is described as the experience of even a momentary flash of recognition of the pure soul (atma darshana), which transforms the practitioner's relationship to spiritual practice permanently. Before right vision, practice is an obligation; after it, practice is a joy.

Samyak Darshana is sometimes described as the result of a single insight that cannot be willed or earned but only received — the soul's sudden recognition of its own true nature. But once received, it must be cultivated through Samyak Jnana and Samyak Charitra. The three are thus interdependent: vision without knowledge leads to misguided enthusiasm; knowledge without vision is mere intellectualism; conduct without either degenerates into empty formalism.

As a mantra and daily intention, Samyak Darshana Jnana Charitra serves as a reminder of the integrated threefold nature of genuine spirituality. Before beginning any practice — study, meditation, ethical reflection — the practitioner can whisper these three words as an orientation: may my seeing be clear, my knowing be accurate, my acting be aligned. The three cover the entirety of human experience: perception, cognition, and action.

Contemporary Jain teachers use this formula in lay practice as a framework for ethical decision-making. When facing a difficult choice, one asks: What is the right vision of this situation (what is truly happening)? What is the right knowledge (what do I actually know vs. assume)? What is the right conduct (what action is most consistent with ahimsa and liberation)? These three questions, systematically applied, constitute a complete Jain ethics of discernment.

translations.en: Right Vision, Right Knowledge, Right Conduct
translations.zh: 正见、正知、正行——耆那教的三重道路
translations.es: Visión Correcta, Conocimiento Correcto, Conducta Correcta

mantras.name: Shuddho Aham
mantras.english: I am pure — the contemplation of the soul's original nature
mantras.transliteration: Śuddho'ham
mantras.abstract:
Shuddho Aham — I am pure — is the central affirmation of Jain contemplative philosophy (adhyatma), the soul's recognition of its own original, uncontaminated nature. In the Jain understanding, the soul (jiva) in its pure state is utterly untouched by karma — the accumulated impressions and matter that appear to bind it. By contemplating and affirming 'I am pure,' the practitioner begins to identify with the soul's actual nature rather than with the karmic overlays that temporarily obscure it.

This practice is related to the Jain concept of nishchaya naya (the absolute viewpoint), which sees the soul as eternally pure, free, omniscient, and blissful — even while the phenomenological or conventional viewpoint (vyavahara naya) acknowledges the reality of karma and the need for practice. Both viewpoints are simultaneously valid; the skill of Jain practice is holding both without contradiction. 'I am pure' (absolute viewpoint) and 'I need to practice to remove karma' (conventional viewpoint) are both true.

Shuddho Aham is the Jain echo of the Vedantic Aham Brahmasmi ('I am Brahman') and the Buddhist recognition of Buddha-nature. In each tradition, the highest practice is a form of recognition — not the achievement of something new but the un-covering of something always already present. The difference in Jain theology is that 'I' remains fully individual: Shuddho Aham does not dissolve into a universal consciousness but recognizes the infinite value and purity of this particular soul.

In the practice of pratikramana (Jain self-examination and confession), Shuddho Aham is recited as the conclusion — after acknowledging all the specific ways one has fallen short of the ideal during the day, one returns to the affirmation of the soul's fundamental purity. The errors do not define the soul; they are karmic accumulations that can be removed. Underneath all the failing, the pure soul remains what it always was.

For practitioners new to Jainism, Shuddho Aham offers an accessible entry into the tradition's most sophisticated spiritual insight. Before knowing anything about Jain cosmology, karma theory, or the 24 Tirthankaras, a person can sit quietly, breathe slowly, and repeat 'I am pure' — allowing the recognition to land not as a theological claim but as a direct, felt experience of the awareness that is always already present, regardless of what thoughts and emotions pass through it.

translations.en: I am pure — the contemplation of the soul's original nature
translations.zh: 我是纯净的——对灵魂本原本性的观想
translations.es: Soy puro — la contemplación de la naturaleza original del alma

mantras.name: Ana B'Koach
mantras.english: Please, with the strength of Your right hand, untie the bundled sins
mantras.original: אָנָּא בְּכֹחַ גְּדֻלַּת יְמִינְךָ תַּתִּיר צְרוּרָה
mantras.transliteration: Ana B'koach gedullat yemincha tatir tzerurah
mantras.abstract:
Ana B'Koach is a mystical prayer composed of 42 words in seven lines, attributed by tradition to the first-century sage Rabbi Nechunia ben HaKanah. Each of the 42 words begins with a letter that, when combined, forms one of the 72 Names of God — the 42-letter divine name (Shem Mem-Bet). This hidden name is considered by Kabbalists to be among the most powerful of all sacred formulas, encoding the mechanism of creation itself.

The forty-two letter name embedded in Ana B'Koach corresponds to the 42 journeys of the Israelites through the desert, which Kabbalists interpret as a map of the soul's journey through different levels of consciousness. Each journey represents a stage of spiritual refinement, and the complete prayer is a recapitulation of the entire spiritual path, from Egypt (the narrowness of ego-consciousness) to the Promised Land (expanded divine awareness).

In Kabbalah, Ana B'Koach is placed at the transition between Friday afternoon and Shabbat, recited as one crosses the threshold from the six days of labor into the sacred seventh day. The 42-letter name is associated with the creative force of the six days of creation, and the prayer invokes that creative power while asking for the release of what is 'bundled' (tzerurah) — the accumulated spiritual heaviness of the week.

The prayer is recited both aloud and in silent contemplation. The Ari (Rabbi Isaac Luria) prescribed reciting Ana B'Koach while meditating on the specific combinations of letters in each line, with each letter activating a different divine quality. His student Chayyim Vital described this practice as direct manipulation of the spiritual structures underlying physical reality.

Modern practitioners use Ana B'Koach as a forgiveness and release prayer. Each line asks for specific spiritual assistance: liberation from constriction, blessing, exaltation, purity, and divine protection. Reciting it slowly at the end of the week or at significant transitions, practitioners report a sense of spiritual clearing — as if the prayer gathers and dissolves accumulated psychic weight, leaving the soul lighter and more spacious.

translations.en: Please, with the strength of Your right hand, untie the bundled sins
translations.zh: 请凭借祢右手力量之伟大，解开那捆绑的罪
translations.es: Por favor, con la fuerza de Tu mano derecha, desata los pecados anudados

mantras.name: And you shall choose life.
mantras.english: And you shall choose life.
mantras.transliteration: Ve-tivcharu ḥayim.
mantras.background: "And you shall choose life" — a phrase whose literal words invoke agency and vitality. Its spare, direct language suggests ancient scriptural roots, inviting practitioners to reflect on intentional living.
mantras.benefits: Affirming life as a conscious choice cultivates agency and resilience. Rooted in Hebrew scripture, this mantra invites purposeful intention, transforming despair into empowered awareness and drawing practitioners toward vitality.
mantras.tags: The provided source material is insufficient to complete this assignment responsibly. The source contains only the mantra phrase itself with no contextual information about its tradition, associated figure, practice type, historical origin, or documented effects. Without this supporting information, I cannot generate the requested 4-12 specific tags without fabricating unsupported claims.

translations.es: Y elegiréis la vida.
translations.ar: وَاخْتَرۡهَا لِلْحَيَوٰة.
translations.bn: এবং তোমরা জীবন নির্বাচন করবে।
translations.de: Ihr sollt das Leben wählen.
translations.en-us: And you shall choose life.
translations.en-gb: And you shall choose life.
translations.fr-fr: Vous choisirez la vie.
translations.he: ובחרת בחיים.
translations.hi: आप जीवन का चयन करोगे।
translations.id: Dan kamu harus memilih kehidupan.
translations.it: Dovrete scegliere la vita.
translations.ja: あなたは、命を選ぶべきである。
translations.ko: 당신은 생명을 선택해야 합니다.
translations.nl: En gij zult het leven kiezen.
translations.pl: I wybierzesz życie.
translations.pt-br: E vós escolherdes a vida.
translations.ru: И вы должны выбрать жизнь.
translations.sv: Och du skall välja livet.
translations.th: คุณจะเลือกชีวิต
translations.tr: Ve siz de yaşamı seçeceksiniz.
translations.uk: І ти повинен обрати життя.
translations.vi: Bạn hãy chọn sự sống.
translations.zh-cn: 你应当选择生命。

mantras.name: Baruch Atah Adonai
mantras.english: Blessed are You, O Lord our God
mantras.transliteration: Baruch Atah Adonai Eloheinu
mantras.abstract:
Baruch Atah Adonai Eloheinu is the opening formula of nearly every Jewish blessing (bracha), occurring over 100 times in the traditional Jewish prayer book (siddur). It is the entry gate into blessing-consciousness — the recognition that ordinary moments of life (eating bread, lighting candles, seeing a rainbow, hearing good news) are occasions for acknowledging divine presence. Through the bracha formula, the mundane and the sacred are consciously unified.

The word 'baruch' (blessed) is grammatically unusual — it is a passive-form adjective applied to God, raising the question of how humans can 'bless' God who is the source of all blessing. The Talmudic response is that human blessing flows back to its divine source as gratitude and recognition; we do not add to God's reality but we acknowledge it, and in that acknowledgment, divine presence is more fully present in the world.

The particular power of the bracha structure lies in its shift of pronouns: 'Baruch Atah' — Blessed are You (second person) — shifts in the next clause to 'Asher kidshanu' — Who has sanctified us (third person). This movement from 'You' to 'Who' mirrors the mystic's experience of approaching divine presence: intimate address suddenly opens into an ineffable vastness that can only be spoken of in the third person.

In contemplative Jewish practice, the discipline of reciting 100 brachot per day is understood as a training of perception: to see 100 moments per day as occasions for gratitude transforms consciousness fundamentally. Each blessing requires a moment of pause, a shift from taking for granted to actively noticing and responding. Over time, this practice develops what Rabbi Abraham Joshua Heschel called 'radical amazement.'

Kabbalists associate the bracha formula with specific sephirot (divine emanations). 'Baruch' corresponds to Keter (Crown), the highest and most hidden; 'Atah' to Chokhmah (Wisdom) and Binah (Understanding); 'Adonai' to Malkhut (Kingdom). The bracha is thus a full traversal of the divine tree, from the hidden source to its manifestation in the world.

translations.en: Blessed are You, O Lord our God
translations.zh: 有福的是祢，我们的主我们的神
translations.es: Bendito seas Tú, Señor nuestro Dios

mantras.name: Blessed are You, Lord our God, King of the universe, who distinguishes between the holy and the profane.
mantras.english: Blessed are You, Lord our God, King of the universe, who distinguishes between the holy and the profane.
mantras.original: Barukh ata Adonai Eloheinu, melekh ha'olam, ha'mavdil bein kodesh l'hol
mantras.transliteration: Barukh ata Adonai Eloheinu Melech ha-olam, hamavdil bein ha-kodesh le-ha-ḥol.
mantras.background: A Jewish blessing from the Havdalah ritual, it marks the boundary between Shabbat's holiness and ordinary time—inviting mindful awareness of life's sacred transitions.
mantras.benefits: Reciting Havdalah's blessing on distinction cultivates mindfulness and sacred awareness, training the mind to perceive boundaries between holy and ordinary—fostering intentionality, gratitude, and conscious living in daily transitions.
mantras.tags: jewish, hebrew, blessing, liturgical, prayer, god, sacred, divine, ritual, praise

translations.es: Bendito eres, Señor, nuestro Dios, Rey del universo, que distingue entre lo santo y lo profano.
translations.ar: مباركٌ أنتَ يا ربّنا وإلهنا، يا ملكَ الكون، الذي تُميّز بين المقدّس والدنس.
translations.bn:
আপনিBlessed are You, Lord our God, King of the universe, who distinguishes between the holy and the profane.

আপনি আমাদের ঈশ্বর, মহাবিশ্বের রাজা, আপনি পবিত্র এবং অপবিত্রের মধ্যে পার্থক্য করেন, আপনার প্রতি আমরা কৃতজ্ঞ।
translations.de: Gepriesen seist Du, Herr, unser Gott, König des Universums, der zwischen dem Heiligen und dem Unheiligen unterscheidet.
translations.en-us: Blessed are You, Lord our God, King of the universe, who discerns between the sacred and the unholy.
translations.en-gb: Blessed are You, Lord our God, King of the universe, who distinguishes between the sacred and the profane.
translations.fr-fr: Béni sois-Tu, Seigneur, notre Dieu, Roi de l'univers, qui sépares le saint du profan.
translations.he: ברוך אתה ה', אלוהינו, מלך היקום, המבדיל בין הקודש והטומאה.
translations.hi: आप धन्य हैं, हे प्रभु हमारे ईश्वर, ब्रह्मांड के राजा, जो पवित्र और अपवित्र के बीच अंतर करते हैं।
translations.id: Terpujilah Engkau, Tuhan kami Allah, Raja alam semesta, yang membedakan antara yang suci dan yang tidak suci.
translations.it: Benedetto tu sei, Signore nostro Dio, Re dell'universo, che distingui tra il sacro e il profano.
translations.ja: 主よ、私たちの神よ、宇宙の王よ。あなたは神聖と俗なるものとの区別をつけられる方。あなたはまことに祝福される方です。
translations.ko: 주님, 우리 하나님, 전 우주의 왕이시여, 거룩한 것과 더러운 것을 구별하시는 주님, 찬양합니다.
translations.nl: Gezegend zij U, Heer onze God, Koning van het universum, die onderscheid maakt tussen het heilige en het onheilige.
translations.pl: Błogosławiony jesteś, Panie, nasz Boże, Królu wszechświata, który odróżniasz święte od profanacji.
translations.pt-br: Bendito és Tu, Senhor nosso Deus, Rei do universo, que distingue entre o sagrado e o profano.
translations.ru: Благословен Ты, Господь, Бог наш, Царь вселенной, Который отличаешь между святым и нечистым.
translations.sv: Välsignad vare Du, Herre, vår Gud, Konung över universum, som skiljer det heliga från det profana.
translations.th: ขอพระเจ้า ผู้เป็นพระเจ้าของเรา พระมหากษัตริย์แห่งจักรวาล จงทรงพร เรา ขอพระองค์ทรงเป็นผู้แยกความศักดิ์สิทธิ์ออกจากสิ่งที่ไม่บริสุทธิ์
translations.tr: Rabbiniz, Tanrımız, evrenin hükümdarı olan, kutsalı ve dünyeviyi ayıran, size şükürler olsun.
translations.uk: Благословенний Ти, Господи, наш Бог, Царю Всесвіту, Який відділяє святе від нечистого.
translations.vi: Kính trọng Đức Chúa, Thượng Đế của chúng con, Vua của vũ trụ, Đấng phân biệt giữa sự thánh và sự không thánh.
translations.zh-cn: 赞美您，主，我们的上帝，宇宙之王，您能分辨圣洁与不洁。

mantras.name: Blessed are You, Lord our God, King of the universe, who has sanctified us with Your commandments and commanded us regarding the commandment of tzitzit.
mantras.english: Blessed are You, Lord our God, King of the universe, who has sanctified us with Your commandments and commanded us regarding the commandment of tzitzit.
mantras.original: Barukh ata Adonai Eloheinu, melekh ha'olam, asher kid'shanu b'mitzvotav v'tzivanu al mitzvat tzitzit
